label: delphi-r1-p1
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the text of the governing statute or constitutional provision, read closely.
text: {"probability":70,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not that a court finds at least a substantial part of the Water Resilience Surcharge violates Article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service/proportionality requirements. The most probable outcome is not wholesale invalidation but severance: the portion of the $0.85 rate attributable to the documented 1.6x marginal-cost differential (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) is likely defensible, while (a) the admitted \"conservation-incentive pricing\" increment above that cost differential and (b) the ~55% of revenue funding rebates that the district concedes mostly benefit non-payers are each independently vulnerable and together likely constitute a \"substantial part\" of the surcharge's economic effect. The district's fallback \"not a property-related fee\" argument is very weak given Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency, and its conservation-mandate defense under Article X, section 2 was already substantially foreclosed by Capistrano's holding that conservation goals do not create an exception to section 6(b)'s cost-basis requirement.","rationales":["The rate study's own admission that $0.85 is derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strictly from cost allocation mirrors the precise defect Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 condemned: tiered pricing must track the actual marginal cost of service at that tier, not function as a behavioral price signal untethered from cost.","The district's concession that the largest revenue bucket (55%, rebates) mostly benefits customers other than the surcharge-payers presents a serious cross-class-subsidization problem under Article XIII D, section 6(b)(3), as that proportionality requirement has been construed in Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372 to bar funding benefits for one customer class with fees collected from a different class.","The documented 1.6x marginal-cost differential (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) likely defends the cost-justified core of the surcharge, so a court is more likely to sever and invalidate only the increment above cost-based pricing plus the cross-subsidized rebate share, rather than strike the entire surcharge -- but that severed portion is plausibly large enough to count as 'substantial part' under the proposition's own framing.","Courts applying Capistrano have generally conducted searching, non-deferential review of whether property-related fees satisfy Article XIII D's proportionality mandate rather than rubber-stamping an agency's rate study, which cuts against the district where its own study concedes non-cost-basis pricing and cross-subsidization on the record.","The district's two defenses are both structurally weak: water delivery charges are squarely 'property-related fees' under Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205, foreclosing the 'not a property-related fee' argument, and Capistrano already rejected the argument that Article X, section 2's conservation mandate overrides Article XIII D's cost-basis requirement, foreclosing the district's primary defense on the merits (though it may still bear on remedy or on characterizing part of the charge as a valid Prop 26 regulatory fee)."],"authorities":["Cal. Const. art. XIII D, § 6(b)(1)-(5)","Cal. Const. art. XIII C, § 1(e) (Prop 26 definition of 'tax' and exclusion for Article XIII D-compliant property-related fees)","Cal. Const. art. X, § 2","Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205","Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372","Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 (verify holding on tiered/inclining rates and cost justification)","Coziahr v. Otay Water District (verify citation/date) -- reportedly upheld tiered rates justified by documented peaking/marginal costs, cutting the other direction on the cost-justified 1.6x portion","Water Code § 375"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether a reviewing court would treat the rebate-funded 55% and the above-cost 'conservation-incentive' increment as severable, independently invalid components sufficient to constitute 'substantial part' invalidation, versus deferring to the rate study's overall reasonableness and upholding the surcharge as a permissible integrated conservation-and-cost-recovery program (especially if the district can recharacterize the rebate/education spending as system-wide demand-reduction that indirectly lowers costs attributable to the paying tier)."}

data.position_summary: More likely than not that a court finds at least a substantial part of the Water Resilience Surcharge violates Article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service/proportionality requirements. The most probable outcome is not wholesale invalidation but severance: the portion of the $0.85 rate attributable to the documented 1.6x marginal-cost differential (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) is likely defensible, while (a) the admitted "conservation-incentive pricing" increment above that cost differential and (b) the ~55% of revenue funding rebates that the district concedes mostly benefit non-payers are each independently vulnerable and together likely constitute a "substantial part" of the surcharge's economic effect. The district's fallback "not a property-related fee" argument is very weak given Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency, and its conservation-mandate defense under Article X, section 2 was already substantially foreclosed by Capistrano's holding that conservation goals do not create an exception to section 6(b)'s cost-basis requirement.
rationales: The rate study's own admission that $0.85 is derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strictly from cost allocation mirrors the precise defect Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 condemned: tiered pricing must track the actual marginal cost of service at that tier, not function as a behavioral price signal untethered from cost.

The district's concession that the largest revenue bucket (55%, rebates) mostly benefits customers other than the surcharge-payers presents a serious cross-class-subsidization problem under Article XIII D, section 6(b)(3), as that proportionality requirement has been construed in Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372 to bar funding benefits for one customer class with fees collected from a different class.

The documented 1.6x marginal-cost differential (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) likely defends the cost-justified core of the surcharge, so a court is more likely to sever and invalidate only the increment above cost-based pricing plus the cross-subsidized rebate share, rather than strike the entire surcharge -- but that severed portion is plausibly large enough to count as 'substantial part' under the proposition's own framing.

Courts applying Capistrano have generally conducted searching, non-deferential review of whether property-related fees satisfy Article XIII D's proportionality mandate rather than rubber-stamping an agency's rate study, which cuts against the district where its own study concedes non-cost-basis pricing and cross-subsidization on the record.

The district's two defenses are both structurally weak: water delivery charges are squarely 'property-related fees' under Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205, foreclosing the 'not a property-related fee' argument, and Capistrano already rejected the argument that Article X, section 2's conservation mandate overrides Article XIII D's cost-basis requirement, foreclosing the district's primary defense on the merits (though it may still bear on remedy or on characterizing part of the charge as a valid Prop 26 regulatory fee).
authorities: Cal. Const. art. XIII C, § 1(e) (Prop 26 definition of 'tax' and exclusion for Article XIII D-compliant property-related fees)
Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493
Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205
Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372
Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 (verify holding on tiered/inclining rates and cost justification)
Coziahr v. Otay Water District (verify citation/date) -- reportedly upheld tiered rates justified by documented peaking/marginal costs, cutting the other direction on the cost-justified 1.6x portion
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a reviewing court would treat the rebate-funded 55% and the above-cost 'conservation-incentive' increment as severable, independently invalid components sufficient to constitute 'substantial part' invalidation, versus deferring to the rate study's overall reasonableness and upholding the surcharge as a permissible integrated conservation-and-cost-recovery program (especially if the district can recharacterize the rebate/education spending as system-wide demand-reduction that indirectly lowers costs attributable to the paying tier).
